questions.question: Explain the Access Matrix model for protection in operating systems with a suitable example.
keyPoints: Access Matrix has rows as domains (subjects) and columns as objects (files/resources)
Each cell contains the access rights that domain has on that object
Can be implemented as Access Control Lists (column-wise) or Capabilities (row-wise)
Example: Domain1 has {read,write} permission on File1 but only {read} on File2

questions.question: Differentiate between Access Control Lists (ACLs) and Capabilities. Give one advantage and one disadvantage of each.
keyPoints: ACL is object-centric; Capability is process-centric
ACL advantage: easy to revoke access; Disadvantage: requires checking object ACL for each access
Capability advantage: fast access verification (process carries rights); Disadvantage: difficult to revoke (capabilities may be copied)
ACL used in UNIX/Windows; Capability used in some specialized systems

questions.question: Explain the UNIX file permission scheme with the help of an example.
keyPoints: Three categories: Owner (user), Group, Others
Three permissions: Read (r), Write (w), Execute (x)
Permissions represented as rwx notation or numeric octal (e.g., 755)
Example: rwxr-x--- means owner has full access, group can read/execute, others have no access
chmod command modifies permissions

questions.question: What is the principle of least privilege? Why is it important in operating system protection?
keyPoints: Principle states users/processes should have minimum privileges needed to complete task
Reduces risk of unauthorized access and damage from errors/malware
Example: A backup program needs only read access, not write access to files
Prevents privilege escalation attacks
Helps in containment of security breaches

questions.question: Explain the difference between Discretionary Access Control (DAC) and Mandatory Access Control (MAC).
keyPoints: DAC: Owners can discretionarily grant/revoke access to their resources (used in UNIX, Windows)
MAC: System enforces access based on security classifications; users cannot override
DAC example: chmod in UNIX allows owner to set permissions
MAC example: Classified military systems with top secret/secret levels
MAC provides stronger security for sensitive applications

questions.question: What are protection domains? How does a process switch between protection domains?
keyPoints: Protection domain defines set of objects and operations allowed on them
Process executes within a domain with specific rights
Domain switching occurs via protected subsystems or privileged operations
Example: User process switches to kernel domain for system calls
Provides controlled privilege escalation

questions.question: Explain the setuid and setgid mechanisms in UNIX with an example.
keyPoints: setuid allows program to run with file owner's effective UID
setgid allows program to run with file owner's effective GID
Example: passwd command has setuid bit; runs as root to modify /etc/passwd
Provides controlled privilege elevation
Security risk if misused - can lead to privilege escalation attacks

questions.question: Describe the Access Matrix model of protection in detail. How is it implemented in practice? Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of its two implementation approaches.
keyPoints: Access Matrix conceptual model: rows=domains, columns=objects, cells=access rights
Implementation 1: Access Control Lists (ACLs) - per-object lists of users and rights
Implementation 2: Capabilities - per-process tokens giving access to objects
ACL Advantages: easy revocation, simple to understand; Disadvantages: overhead on each access
Capability Advantages: efficient access check; Disadvantages: revocation is complex
Most systems use hybrid approaches or primarily ACLs (UNIX, Windows)

questions.question: Explain in detail the various file permission mechanisms in UNIX/Linux operating systems. How would you set appropriate permissions for: (a) A private file (b) A shared folder for a group (c) A public executable program?
keyPoints: Basic permissions: Owner/Group/Others with rwx bits
Special permissions: setuid, setgid, sticky bit
Numeric notation: r=4, w=2, x=1
For private file (owner only): chmod 600 or chmod u=rw,g=,o=
For shared folder: chmod 770 or chmod 775 for group access with setgid
For public executable: chmod 755 or chmod 775
Detailed explanation of each permission setting with rationale

questions.question: Discuss the various access control models used in operating systems for protection. Compare DAC, MAC, and RBAC with examples of where each is used.
keyPoints: DAC (Discretionary Access Control): Owner-controlled, used in UNIX, Windows - users grant access at will
MAC (Mandatory Access Control): System-enforced based on classifications, used in military, classified systems
RBAC (Role-Based Access Control): Access via roles not users, used in organizations (admin, manager, employee)
Comparison: DAC flexible but less secure; MAC very secure but inflexible; RBAC balances both
Examples: UNIX/DAC; SELinux/MAC; Corporate systems/RBAC

questions.question: What is protection in operating systems? Explain the various goals of protection and discuss any two mechanisms used to implement protection in file systems.
keyPoints: Protection ensures authorized access to system resources
Goals: prevent unauthorized access, ensure data integrity, enable controlled sharing, maintain system stability
Mechanism 1: Access Control Lists (ACLs) - file-centric permission lists
Mechanism 2: Capabilities - process-centric tokens
Other mechanisms: Passwords, encryption, authentication
Discuss implementation details of chosen mechanisms
